segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with a dark, atmospheric shot of a runner’s silhouette on a city street, accompanied by an upbeat, slightly dramatic score. A male voice recounts Nike's humble beginnings, stating, "First year sales were 1964, we sold $8,000. We made a $240 profit." This transitions to a close-up of a red Nike sneaker with a glowing swoosh, as a female voice highlights the brand's immense growth: "Today, his company Nike has grown in $30 billion in sales last year." The scene shifts to a man in a suit speaking with intensity against blurred Nike branding, followed by a dramatic red and black visual effect emphasizing a glowing Nike logo on a sneaker. An intense, driving musical beat builds with a whoosh sound as a sports announcer exclaims, "Michael Jordan in the NBA!", aligning with the visual of a young man in a 'BULLS' jersey standing confidently on a basketball court. The title 'Just Do It: The Inside Story of Nike's Empire' appears, sponsored by Shopify, with a calm male voice thanking them for sponsoring the video. Atmospheric, slightly eerie music and a scratching sound introduce the chapter title 'FINDING PURPOSE' over sketches of sneakers. A silhouette of a man in distress appears against a yellow background with the word 'NIGHTMARE,' followed by 'EXCEPT' and 'Reality.' A distinct male narrator begins a storytelling segment, explaining, "Phil Knight suddenly woke up. He just been having a nightmare where his life had no purpose... except it wasn't just a dream. This was his reality." The narrator continues, detailing how Phil, unsure what to do, went to the University of Oregon, where he studied journalism and developed his interest in running. The University of Oregon stadium is shown with a runner on a track, introducing Bill Bowerman, labeled as a 'Middle-Distance Runner' in text, and described by the narrator as "the track coach Bill Bowerman, a famous coach in America who trained Olympic athletes." A montage of runners, historical photos, and a close-up of a classic Nike sneaker reinforces the brand’s athletic roots, as the narrator explains Phil trained under Bowerman and they struck up a great friendship, which would later become "the foundation of the biggest sports company in the world." The word 'JOURNALISM' appears over a stadium, linking to Phil's studies. The segment ends with a man in a suit standing by a window, gazing at a vintage photo of runners, symbolizing reflection on Nike’s history.

A sepia-toned portrait of Phil in a collared shirt, accompanied by text 'He just hadn’t got to the level needed' and 'HE FELT LOST,' reinforces his emotional struggle. The scene dramatically shifts to a yellow-toned background with marching soldiers and helicopters, with text 'FRUSTRATED ANGRY,' as the narrator recounts Phil enlisting in the army and feeling frustrated. A business-themed montage with a lightbulb icon and 'NEW BUSINESS' text leads to a hand writing a pivotal question on paper, which the narrator reads aloud: "Can Japanese Sports Shoes Do to German Sports Shoes What Japanese Cameras Did?" This is followed by visuals of cameras on tripods and a map of Germany with Nikon and Canon flags, illustrating how Japanese camera companies had replaced German ones, as the narrator explains. The narrative then transitions to a map of the U.S. with Nike flags and a purple Nike sneaker with 'running shoes' text, as the narrator wonders if the same disruption could happen with running shoes. The segment concludes with a split screen showing PUMA and adidas logos over American and German flags, representing the leading German running shoe brands, and a silhouette of a man raising his arm in triumph against a stock market backdrop, symbolizing the eventual market success of Phil's vision. Throughout, a consistent male narrator guides the story, accompanied by background music.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with a dynamic montage of abstract visuals, including a Japanese flag and a close-up of a sneaker, overlaid with text stating 'He also knew that it would be cheaper to produce shoes in Japan as well.' A clear male narrator explains that Phil had heard the Japanese were experimenting with new shoe designs and that production would be cheaper there. The scene transitions to a classroom setting with a chalkboard displaying logos of PUMA, adidas, Canon, and Nikon, connected by red pins and dashed lines, symbolizing a strategic network, as the narrator details Phil's extreme investment in this business class assignment, genuinely believing in the opportunity to import Japanese shoes into the American market. Maps of Japan ('Japanese shoes') and the United States ('American market') appear, illustrating this global business strategy. A man in a collared shirt, identified by the narrator as Phil, is shown covering his face in frustration or realization, while another man gestures dramatically in the background, as the narrator recounts how Phil's idea was met with skepticism by his classmates and friends. A glowing lightbulb with a sneaker inside, surrounded by mathematical equations, emphasizes innovation. Phil reappears with a determined expression, as the text 'himself' appears, and the narrator questions the difficulty of pursuing this idea alone, given Phil's lack of contacts or knowledge about importing and selling. A bold red background with 'JAPAN' and 'Japanese shoes $$$' highlights cost and market potential. The video then cuts to a black screen with the text 'Could he really JUST DO IT?', accompanied by the narrator's direct question, "So, could he really just do it?", as the background music swells and then fades. After a brief silence, the distinct sound of a pen writing is heard, followed by a sharp click and a whoosh, leading to a title card: 'CHAPTER 2: A TRIP TO THE FUTURE.' The final scenes show graduates tossing caps in front of the University of Oregon Stadium, with the year '1962' displayed, as the narrator explains that after graduating from business school in 1962, Phil found himself back home, living with his parents, feeling that familiar sense of his life just passing him by, all set to a dramatic, melancholic musical score.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with a man, identified by the narrator as Phil, in a suit sitting contemplatively on a yoga mat, head in hands, against a vibrant abstract background of purple and red light streaks, as the narrator explains Phil needed to do something meaningful. The scene transitions to a black and white close-up of Phil with a hand covering his face, as the word 'MAGIC' appears, aligning with the narrator's description of Phil realizing he couldn't just wait for a 'magic opportunity.' Phil is then seen holding a glowing red orb, symbolizing his powerful vision, while a photographer captures the moment, as the narrator details Phil's decision to create an opportunity for himself and embark on a world trip with a friend. The orb reappears in a red circle with text, reinforcing Phil's 'crazy idea' of bringing Japanese shoes to the American market, which he hoped to make 'into a reality' by visiting Japan. Their journey is visually represented by iconic landmarks like the Colosseum in Rome and the Temple of Nike in Greece, which the narrator explicitly mentions as places they visited, linking to the goddess of victory. The narrative dramatically shifts to a tropical Hawaiian beach, with the text 'That’s where things CHANGED!' as two people toast, accompanied by upbeat, energetic, slightly tropical music with wave sound effects, signifying a turning point. A surfer rides a wave, and a man in a Hawaiian shirt stands on a dock, embracing the new environment. The scene returns to Phil in his suit, now labeled 'Phil was pretty shy,' against a red grid, with a silhouette of him walking away with a suitcase, as the narrator reveals Phil's shyness and poor performance at a sales job selling encyclopedias door-to-door in Honolulu, which he and his friend took to extend their stay in 'Hawaiian paradise.' The word 'LEAVE' appears, followed by 'REVEAL,' as the narrator states Phil decided it was 'time to leave,' while his friend stayed behind. The final frame shows Phil looking conflicted with a hand on his head, as 'REVEAL' remains on screen, capturing his internal struggle and impending decision.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with a dramatic, stylized shot of the Man in Suit, identified as 'Phil,' against a red and black grid, as a male narrator states that Phil "already knew exactly where he wanted to go next." The focus shifts to a purple-lit retail display of Onitsuka Tiger sneakers, with the brand logo prominent, as the narrator details how Phil discovered a particular running shoe in Kobe in 1962, noting the brand was called 'Tiger' and manufactured by 'Onitsuka.' Text overlays emphasize 'two main criteria: quality' and 'me,' aligning with the narrator's explanation that the shoes were "clearly high-quality" yet "relatively cheap." Phil is then shown on the phone, looking nervous and excited, with the Onitsuka Tiger logo superimposed, as the narrator reveals he "cold-called the owners" to arrange a meeting. The video concludes with a group of confident Asian business professionals in front of a large U.S. dollar bill, symbolizing success, as the Onitsuka Tiger logo and Phil’s image fade in and out, while the narrator explains Phil, despite "zero expertise," introduced himself as an American shoe distributor to market their shoes in America, and that Onitsuka "had been looking for a way."

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with a grayscale image of Phil, the Man in Suit, gesturing expansively against a purple background, as a narrator introduces "Blue Ribbon Sports of Portland, Oregon," explaining that Phil "just kind of blurted the name out of nowhere" and "never expected to get this far." A split-screen shows Phil and a younger man in professional settings, suggesting strategic planning, while the narrator details Phil's increasing confidence as he pitched the American market and the unique "Onitsuka's Tiger shoes." A close-up of a white Onitsuka Tiger sneaker with blue and red stripes appears, followed by its logo, as the narrator describes the Japanese company's eagerness to make Phil their distribution partner in the US, to which he "obviously agreed." A group of diverse professionals, including a smiling Asian man, celebrate, visually confirming the agreement, as a map of the United States pinned with a red thumbtack and images of Onitsuka Tiger sneakers appear, with text declaring Phil's "crazy idea was about to become a reality." The scene fades to black, transitioning to a title card: 'CHAPTER 3: ONE BECOMES TWO.' A clock ticking sound accompanies the visual of a clock face with '1 MONTH' and a calendar showing January 1, as the narrator counts "One month, two months, three months, four months," emphasizing that "no shoe samples arrived." Phil reappears with a distressed, fragmented visual effect, having returned to America "so excited and optimistic," only to be confused by the delay. A close-up shows a hand writing 'WHAT WAS GOING ON WITH THE SAMPLE,' directly following the narrator's mention that Phil "wrote to them," with a torn envelope displaying '50 DOLLARS' hinting at the financial or logistical crux of the situation.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with a close-up of a torn envelope containing a handwritten note that reads 'Shoes coming, In just a little more days,' which the narrator reads aloud, explaining Phil was waiting for samples he'd paid $50 for. The scene transitions to a sepia-toned portrait of a man with a mustache and cap, identified as Phil's dad, who chuckles and states, "Son, that 50 bucks is long gone," as the text 'SON, THAT FIFTY BUCKS IS LONG GONE' overlays the image, suggesting a financial loss. A bold question, 'HAD PHIL REALLY BEEN SCAMMED?' appears, posed by the narrator, setting a tone of doubt. The narrative shifts to a modern-day delivery woman in a blue cap and white shirt, driving a van filled with shoe boxes, delivering them to a building, as the narrator announces that "12 sample pairs of shoes from Japan arrived on his doorstep." Inside, a man in a suit receives the boxes with satisfaction, confirmed by the narrator stating, "They were just like he'd hoped," while the text 'TWELVE SAMPLE PAIRS OF SHOES' appears. The scene cuts to a historical figure, Bill Bowerman, shown in a sepia portrait, with the narrator explaining Phil went to see his old running coach, who "was highly respected in the world of running" and "obsessed with improving athletes' shoes." Bowerman is then seen at the University of Oregon, cutting open a shoe, with the text 'CUT THEM OPEN MAKE ADJUSTMENTS,' as the narrator details how he would "take student shoes and cut them open and make adjustments." An animated diagram of a shoe highlights modifications like 'Adding more cushion or using more lightweight materials,' which the narrator describes as examples of Bowerman's work. A runner on a track is shown in pain, with the text 'JUST MADE THEIR FEET PAINFUL,' contrasting with the narrator's comment that sometimes experiments "just made their feet painful." This is followed by a shot of a woman running comfortably, with the text 'IMPROVED THE SHOES BETTER OR MORE COMFORTABLE,' as the narrator concludes that "quite often, Bowerman improved the shoes and made them better or more comfortable for running." The segment ends with a man in a white shirt and cap, smiling, as the word 'BETTER' appears, symbolizing successful innovation.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with a dynamic montage of historical runners in sepia tones, as a narrator explains how Bill Bowerman "really knew his stuff when it came to running shoes," and how Phil had been keen to hear his opinion on his "new Japanese running shoes." A close-up of a blue Asics sneaker with a white logo appears, followed by a handshake between two men in suits, symbolizing the business agreement after Bowerman liked the sample shoes so much he offered to be Phil's business partner. The scene transitions to a man in a suit examining a shoe, with text 'PERFECT PARTNER' and 'TRAINING ATHLETES' appearing over abstract purple and gold paint splashes, as the narrator elaborates that Bowerman was indeed the "perfect partner," having spent his life training athletes and being obsessed with track shoe construction. The narrative shifts to a storefront labeled 'BLUE RIBBON SPORTS TIGER SHOES', indicating the launch of the new company, Blue Ribbon Sports, co-founded by Phil and Bowerman, each investing $500. A conveyor belt of shoes and text overlays revealing '300 PAIRS OF SHOES' at '$3.33 A PAIR' emphasize the initial small-scale operation and affordability, as the narrator confirms this money was used to order 300 pairs from Japan at roughly $3.33 per pair. The story progresses with a map of the U.S. highlighting 'EXCLUSIVE DISTRIBUTOR' status, as Phil wrote to Onitsuka asking for Blue Ribbon Sports to be the exclusive distributor of these Tiger shoes in the Western United States. This is followed by a cargo ship labeled 'IN THE YEAR 1964' carrying a shipment of '300 TIGER SHOES', which the narrator confirms arrived from Japan in April 1964. The final frames show a man in a suit with a concerned expression, overlaid with text 'THERE WAS JUST ONE PROBLEM' and 'IS ANYWHERE TO SELL THE SHOES', as the narrator concludes that while Phil now had a supplier, a partner, and shoes, "What Phil didn't have was anywhere to sell the shoes." The segment ends with a close-up of a shoe with a purple circle, symbolizing the core product and the looming business hurdle.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with a purple Nike sneaker prominently displayed, overlaid with the text 'THEY DIDN’T TAKE PHIL SERIOUSLY,' as a male narrator explains how Phil Knight's initial attempts to sell his shoes to local sporting goods stores were met with rejection, stating, "they didn't take Phil seriously" and "had no need for another track shoe." A diagram appears, showing Phil Knight's face connected to rejected stores, leading him to realize he "was going to have to sell these shoes himself." A sepia-toned Phil, now with white hair, holds a shoe as the text 'SELL THESE SHOES' appears, emphasizing his determination. The title 'CHAPTER 4: BLUERIBBON' emerges, surrounded by sneaker sketches, marking a new phase. The narrative shifts to Phil selling shoes from the trunk of his car in front of a Kmart sign, with the narrator detailing how he "began going to track meets and running clubs, and started selling the shoes from the trunk of his car." A montage of runners on a track, accompanied by the text 'TO RUNNERS,' shows Phil enthusiastically presenting the shoes to athletes, coaches, and fans. This grassroots effort, combined with his commitment, led to selling "all 300 pairs within just 3 months," highlighted by a smiling man in sunglasses and bold text. The narrator draws a parallel to Phil's past, noting that while he "failed horribly" selling encyclopedias door-to-door, selling these running shoes "seemed strangely easy." A close-up of a hand holding a purple Nike sneaker appears with text emphasizing 'HIS PERSONAL BELIEF IN THE PRODUCT' and how it 'MADE PEOPLE WANNA BUY THEM,' as the narrator confirms customers could "sense his personal belief in the product," making them want to buy. The segment concludes with a calendar showing 'FIRST YEAR' and a clock face, followed by a 'BLUE RIBBON SPORTS TIGER SHOES' storefront, where money rains down, symbolizing financial success. The narrator reveals that "by the end of their first year, Blue Ribbon Sports had sold 8,000" pairs, marking a triumphant breakthrough. Throughout, a light, evolving instrumental background music underscores the narrative, shifting from skepticism to determination and finally to success, punctuated by subtle sound effects.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with a dynamic montage of money raining over a cargo ship labeled 'MAERSK,' symbolizing global commerce and financial success, as the narrator explains Phil used his earnings to order "900 more pairs from Japan," aligning with the bold text '900 MORE.' The scene transitions to a man in a suit holding a tablet, with the text 'MOSTLY EX-RUNNERS' appearing, as the narrator details the recruitment of sales reps, mostly former athletes who "shared their vision" and worked "solely on commission." A close-up of hands counting money is shown with the text 'WHO SHARED THE VISION,' emphasizing alignment and purpose, while the narrator specifies they earned "$2 for every pair of shoes they sold," reinforced by the repeated '$2' text. The narrative shifts to a woman in a white shirt, then a map of the U.S. with a dotted line from Philadelphia to California appears, labeled 'ALLOWED PHI,' as the narrator describes their expansion across America and how their success led to a business loan, allowing them to move "Blue Ribbon Sports out of Phil's parents' basement and into its first real office." This is followed by a group of excited people celebrating and a modern office environment with digital icons, labeled 'FULL-TIME JOB,' showing professional growth. A man in a plaid shirt is shown with the text 'JEFF JOHNSON,' identified by the narrator as "the company's first full-time employee" hired because Bowerman "still had a full-time job," and notably, "the man who would later go on to come up with the company's iconic name, Nike." The video then highlights a man with white hair, with text 'THEY WERE STILL CALLED BLUE RIBBON SPORTS,' as the narrator confirms that "for now, they were still called Blue Ribbon Sports." A bar graph showing sales growth from 1964 to 1966 appears, with rising orange bars and a purple line, illustrating financial success, as the narrator states that "with sales rising rapidly, in 1966, they were able to open their own retail store to sell their shoes." The scene transitions to a store interior with the 'BLUE RIBBON SPORTS' logo, where people are browsing, and text 'DESIGNED THE STORE TO BE' appears, emphasizing strategic retail design, as the narrator explains they "designed the store to be a sanctuary for runners," a place to "hang out and talk" and browse books, which "helped them attract a lot of attention." A man in a suit is shown with text 'WORKED ON THE BUSINESS SIDE,' highlighting operational focus, as the narrator clarifies that "Phil and the new employee Johnson worked on the business side of the operations," while "Bowerman was handling the creative" aspects. The video ends with a close-up of a hand sketching shoe designs on paper, labeled 'Shoe Design Ideas,' with a red circle and scissors, symbolizing innovation and product development.

A hand holds the green book 'JOGGING' by William J. Bowerman & W.E. Harris, M.D., as the narrator highlights that it 'surprisingly sold over a million copies and helped kickstart the popularity of jogging.' (implying $300,000).

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with a collage of vintage black-and-white and sepia-toned images, including runners, a man in a suit holding a shoe, and a storefront labeled 'BLUE RIBBON SPORTS TIGER SHOES,' overlaid with bold orange text stating '$300,000' and 'THAT BROUGHT SUCCESS TO BOTH PARTIES.' A male narrator immediately states, "300,000 dollars," explaining that "business was booming, and everything seemed to be going great between Phil's company, Blue Ribbon Sports, and Onitsuka in Japan." He continues, "Together, they'd created a partnership that brought success to both parties." The scene transitions to a purple-tinted image of a man in a suit and cap, juxtaposed with a sepia-toned man in a suit holding a shoe, symbolizing the partnership's success. The narrative then shifts dramatically to a green-tinted, digital-themed sequence featuring the Nike logo with question marks, followed by a silhouette of a man facing a glowing Earth and a cascade of money, symbolizing global ambition and financial growth. An ominous background music begins as the narrator reveals, "But what Phil didn't know was that Onitsuka was planning to stab them in the back." The music fades, and the narrator shifts to a reflective tone, stating, "Hearing this story of Nike shows how difficult it was for entrepreneurs to execute on their ideas back then. Phil literally started by selling shoes out of his car." The background music becomes upbeat and modern as the narrator introduces a solution: "But now, anyone with an idea for a product can start selling online quickly and easily with Shopify." The video cuts to a modern, brightly lit home office where a woman smiles at her laptop, transitioning to a screen displaying Shopify’s interface with the tagline 'If you can dream it, you can sell it with Shopify,' highlighting ease of online business creation. The narrator elaborates, "Shopify allows anyone, even those with no technical skills at all, to create, grow, and manage a business." He adds, "What I love about Shopify is how much time it saves you. In just a few clicks, you can create a stunning, professional-looking online store that's completely tailored to your business, and begin selling your products and receiving payments." The sequence continues with a man in glasses typing on a laptop, surrounded by floating dollar bills and green digital effects, reinforcing the theme of online entrepreneurship. The narrator shares a personal goal: "For me, having an online business had always been my goal. I wanted to be able to travel anywhere in the world and know that wherever I..." The final frames show a white silhouette of a man against a green background of money, with a shopping bag icon appearing over his head, symbolizing the culmination of an online business journey. The Shopify logo appears prominently, tying the narrative to the platform’s role in enabling success.

segments.integrated_caption: The minute opens with an upbeat, electronic background track as a male voiceover promotes Shopify, stating his business is making money thanks to the platform. Visually, a white silhouette of a man against a green, money-filled background with the Shopify logo transitions to a vibrant purple neon outline, followed by a close-up of hands counting money near a laptop. The scene then shows a green gradient screen with the Shopify logo and a call to action: 'CLICK THE LINK IN THE DESCRIPTION' with downward arrows, as the voiceover encourages viewers to start their own business with Shopify. A dramatic transition sound marks the shift to 'CHAPTER 5: BETRAYAL,' a title card set against sneaker sketches. The narrative continues with a male voiceover explaining that Phil, represented by the Man with White Hair on a split-screen with the Man in Suit holding an Onitsuka Tiger shoe, asked Onitsuka if Blue Ribbon Sports could be the exclusive distributor for Tiger shoes across all of America. A map of North America with the U.S. highlighted in orange flashes 'NO!' as the narrator explains Onitsuka's initial rejection, believing Phil's company wasn't big or established enough, a point reinforced by towering skyscrapers under an orange sky with explanatory text. The narrator then recounts Phil's immediate, albeit false, claim, "But we do have offices on the East Coast," as the Man with White Hair reappears over a world map with the U.S. highlighted in yellow, followed by a bold red 'THEY DIDN'T' text overlay. Despite this, the Man in Suit is shown again with text stating 'ONITSUKA BELIEVED PHIL' and the Blue Ribbon Sports logo, as the narrator confirms Onitsuka was impressed and struck a deal for Blue Ribbon to be the exclusive US partner. The segment concludes with the Man with White Hair, a large '20,000' and Onitsuka Tiger shoes, symbolizing the successful sales milestone amidst the building tension of betrayal.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with a purple-tinted map of the United States, overlaid with location pins and shipping boxes, symbolizing a distribution network for an order of "20,000 more pairs" of shoes. A narrator explains that an Onitsuka executive promised to ship these to Blue Ribbon's East Coast office, which, in reality, didn't exist. The scene transitions to a man in a dark suit and red tie, identified by the narrator as Phil, in a dimly lit office, as promotional flyers for 'Blue Ribbon Sports' appear with bold text: 'INVEST IN BRS NOW!' The narrator details how Phil sent these flyers to raise money for new offices, describing the company as a fast-growing shoe business looking to expand, where people could buy shares. However, the response was nonexistent—'NOBODY AT ALL RESPONDED'—leading to a moment of humiliation, visually represented by a man covering his face, while the narrator emphasizes the worrying lack of belief in their business. The narrative shifts to an older couple in a modest home, counting cash in bins, identified by the narrator as the parents of early employee Bob Woodell, who invested $8,000—their "entire life savings"—to help the company, despite a visual of '$95,000' highlighted in front of a bank facade. The narrator explains Phil's reluctance to take their money, but banks were refusing further loans, leaving him no other choice. The couple is then shown in an office meeting, while a man in a plaid shirt, Jeff Johnson, appears frustrated and later in a backyard, visibly distressed. The segment ends with a split-screen of two older men, one with white hair and a mustache, the other with a stern expression, with a white Onitsuka Tiger sneaker superimposed between them, linking the financial hardship to the product and the initial supplier. A dramatic instrumental score underscores the entire narrative.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with a dramatic, suspenseful background score as a male narrator explains the initial partnership. A split-screen shows two older men, identified as Phil Knight and Bill Bowerman, one with white hair and a mustache, the other with a stern expression. Overlaid with a white Onitsuka Tiger sneaker and text, the narrator states they 'loved the shoes created by Onitsuka' and 'respected the quality of the Japanese brand,' making it seem 'win-win for both sides.' The scene transitions to a patterned background of shoe silhouettes with the 'BRS' logo and 'BLUE RIBBON SPORTS' text, followed by an orange map of the U.S. with 'Onitsuka Tiger' written across it, as the narrator details how Onitsuka became a 'formidable brand in the United States' thanks to Blue Ribbon Sports. A man in a suit, Phil, appears in a dimly lit, purple-tinted room, speaking with concern, followed by another man in a blue shirt gesturing thoughtfully, as the narrator reveals that despite Phil's belief in Onitsuka, 'they had some problems' that were 'about to get even bigger.' A Jenga tower labeled with 'A', 'B', 'BB', 'BBB' symbolizes this instability, with text explaining that Blue Ribbon Sports' initial success led to rapid expansion. A storefront with a 'BLUE RIBBON SPORTS TIGER SHOES' sign appears, followed by the white-haired man (Phil) reappearing with text about cash flow risks, as the narrator explains Phil 'constantly had to worry about cash flow' to support new stores and employees. A man in a tan jacket walks through a dark, industrial setting with 'HIRE MORE STAFF' text, aligning with Phil's need to 'get more facilities, hire more staff, and order more shoes.' The Onitsuka Tiger sneaker reappears over a purple background with shoe silhouettes, then Phil, the man in a suit, gestures passionately as text states 'AND THEY WERE SELLING OUT FASTER THAN THEY COULD KEEP UP WITH,' with the narrator specifically mentioning the 'Cortez' model's popularity. A map of the U.S. and Japan appears with the sneaker flying between them, as the narrator explains Phil 'repeatedly tried to have Onitsuka send the shoes faster.' The final shot shows Phil holding a shoe with text 'ONITSUKA HAD BEEN SELLING TO THEIR JAPANESE CUSTOMERS FIRST AS THE PRIORITY,' followed by a cargo ship at sea with text 'AND SIMPLY IMPORTING WHATEVER WAS LEFT OVER TO THE US,' highlighting the supply chain conflict.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with a dimly lit, purple-tinted close-up of the Man in Suit, identified by the narrator as Phil, his expression tense, as text overlays state 'THERE WASN’T REALLY MUCH PHIL COULD DO,' a sentiment echoed by the narrator who states, "It was unfair, but there wasn't really much Phil could do." The scene shifts to a dark warehouse filled with shoe boxes, with the word 'SUGGESTIONS' in bold orange, followed by a shot of Phil in a meeting, with text indicating 'THAT PHIL AND BOWERMAN SHARED WITH THEM.' A sepia-toned portrait of Bill Bowerman appears against a background of shoe sketches, with text noting his efforts to improve designs, as the narrator explains that Onitsuka was slow to produce the innovations Phil and Bowerman shared, sometimes completely ignoring their suggestions, which "infuriated Bowerman, who'd been working hard on new designs and improvements." The narrative continues with a formal dinner scene where two men in suits converse, overlaid with text about renewing the contract, as Phil, hoping to improve the situation, is then shown in a dark, blue-lit setting, with text revealing he arranged another meeting with Onitsuka. A silhouette of a man in a suit appears against a purple background, visually labeled 'THE MAN NAMED KITAGAWA,' but the narrator identifies him as "A man named Katami," describing the meeting as 'STRANGE' and noting that Katami said "Blue Ribbon sales were disappointing" and showed a lack of interest in renewing the deal. The scene transitions to a patterned shoe background with text about disappointing sales, then to a dimly lit office where Phil looks concerned, with text stating he felt his company had earned the contract, a feeling the narrator confirms, stating Phil "couldn't understand it" and "felt his company had definitely earned the contract extension." A map of the U.S. in orange appears with the Onitsuka Tiger logo, followed by a man in a suit standing before a fiery backdrop, with text about introducing the Tiger brand in America, as the narrator explains Onitsuka's success was "down to Blue Ribbon Sports" who introduced the "Tiger brand in America," with sales "growing every year." A close-up of a man in a suit shows text about 70% of American runners wearing Onitsuka shoes, with Phil having shown them a survey to prove it, followed by a shoe graphic and text about Bowerman’s design contributions, which the narrator confirms, adding that Bowerman "had helped improve their shoe designs." The scene shifts to a dark hallway with silhouettes walking, with text revealing Onitsuka’s plans to partner with other distributors, a suspicion Phil had already harbored, according to the narrator. The segment ends with a man in a tuxedo looking alarmed, with a red warning triangle and text 'SOMETHING WASN’T RIGHT,' emphasizing the growing sense of betrayal and conflict, as the narrator's voice fades, stating it was "becoming increasingly..."

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with a dimly lit office scene where a man in a suit, identified as Phil Knight, is seen in a tense meeting, reflecting the growing strain in the business relationship, as a voiceover states that 'something wasn't right.' After 'Katami left the room,' Phil took a folder from his briefcase. The narrative shifts to a series of animated graphics: a stack of 'SHOE DISTRIBUTOR' documents, which the voiceover specifies contained 'a list of 18 athletic shoe distributors across the US,' followed by a calendar labeled 'APPOINTMENTS,' symbolizing 'a schedule of appointments to meet with them.' A man in a suit, likely a key Onitsuka executive, is shown in front of a chalkboard filled with diagrams and notes, with text overlay stating 'IT WAS NOW CLEAR THAT ONITSUKA WAS SECRETLY PLOTTING TO REPLACE BLUE RIBBON,' a betrayal confirmed by the voiceover stating Onitsuka was 'secretly plotting to replace Blue Ribbon and find a new distributor.' A map of the USA in orange appears with the Onitsuka Tiger logo, emphasizing the brand's dominance and the conflict over distribution, as the voiceover explains Onitsuka had 'gained a significant foothold in the United States.' Text 'THANKS TO BLUE RIBBON'S EFFORT' appears, followed by silhouettes of executives with the message 'THE ONITSUKA EXECUTIVES DECIDED THAT THEY NEEDED TO MOVE ON TO EXPAND,' with the voiceover elaborating that 'the Onitsuka executives decided that they needed to move on to more experienced distributors to expand and increase their revenue.' A close-up of a man in a blue shirt, identified by the voiceover as 'Phil,' shows him in deep thought, reflecting the emotional weight of the situation, as the voiceover describes him as 'outraged' and 'hurt,' and unable to 'believe this was happening.' The narrative escalates with the Onitsuka Tiger logo and text stating 'EITHER WE BUY 51% OF BLUE RIBBON SPORTS OR WE MAKE DEALS WITH OTHER DISTRIBUTION PARTNERS,' presenting the ultimatum, which the voiceover explicitly states was given by 'the Japanese brand.' The Man in Suit (Phil Knight) is shown in a dimly lit setting, with text 'PHIL KNIGHT IMMEDIATELY REJECTED THE OFFER TO BUY HIS COMPANY,' highlighting his defiance, as the voiceover confirms 'Phil Knight immediately rejected their offer to buy his company.' The scene transitions to a handshake graphic between Onitsuka Tiger and distribution partners, with a man in a suit holding an Onitsuka Tiger shoe, symbolizing the brand's new alliances, which the voiceover describes as making 'deals with other distribution partners.' The final frame shows a fiery explosion with the text 'THEN IT WAS TIME FOR WAR,' marking the beginning of a direct confrontation between Onitsuka and Blue Ribbon Sports, accompanied by an explosion sound effect and the voiceover declaring, 'then it was time for war.' The lighting throughout is predominantly purple and dark, creating a dramatic and tense atmosphere, with text overlays in bold yellow and orange to emphasize key plot points.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with an ominous, low-frequency hum accompanying a bold title card, 'CHAPTER 6: DON’T GET ANGRY, GET EVEN,' set against a dark background with faint sneaker sketches, establishing a tone of retaliation and strategic resolve. A male narrator explains that it took a while for 'Phil,' a Man in Suit seen gazing contemplatively at a cityscape from a dimly lit office, to get over the shock of a meeting, but he ultimately decided to 'strike back.' The narrator continues, detailing how 'Phil and Bowerman' realized that losing the Onitsuka Tiger brand, specifically the 'Cortez' (Onitsuka's best-selling shoe designed by Bowerman), wasn't the end of the world but an opportunity to be more than just a distributor. The scene shifts to a shadowy shoe warehouse and a close-up of the white Onitsuka Tiger sneaker, followed by a vintage 'BLUE RIBBON SPORTS TIGER SHOES' storefront, as the narrator questions why Blue Ribbon couldn't just make and sell their own shoes. A cargo ship sails across a purple-tinted sea, overlaid with text about importing 'whatever was left over,' visually underscoring the distribution issues that making their own shoes would resolve, as the narrator explains they wouldn't have to wait for shipments and could make as many as they wanted. The two co-founders, Phil and Bowerman, are then seen in a Boardroom Meeting, gathering their team to work on creating their 'own original shoes' instead of selling Onitsuka's. segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens in a dimly lit boardroom where Phil, the Man in Suit, wearing a dark suit and patterned tie, gravely addresses his employees. He announces, "Our partnership with Onitsuka is over, and we're completely on our own now," a statement reinforced by on-screen text. Phil reveals they are working on a new line called Nike but admits, "there are big problems with the quality," and with limited time, "there's no margin for error." The camera cuts between close-ups of Phil and concerned reactions from seated executives, including a man with gray hair and another with dark hair and a red tie, who initially appear slumped and defeated, fearing the end of Blue Ribbon Sports. Realizing he needs to inspire his team, Phil pivots his speech, declaring, "This is the moment we've been waiting for," as animated icons symbolizing logistics, product, and innovation flash across the screen. He passionately explains how Onitsuka's late deliveries, mixed-up orders, and refusal to implement design ideas held them back, urging his team, "Let's not look at this as a crisis. Let's look at this as our liberation." A wave of relief washes over the now attentive employees, including a man with glasses and a red tie, as the mood shifts. The scene then transitions to a dimly lit workshop, emphasizing hands-on production and quality control, where a Shoe Workshop Worker in a striped shirt and apron operates a sewing machine, overseen by a Man in a Red Jacket, symbolizing the collective effort to make the new Nike brand successful.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with a close-up of a legal document, identified by a narrator's serious voice as "the bill from the US Custom Service," prominently displaying 'YOU OWE: $25 MILLION DOLLARS,' immediately establishing a high-stakes financial crisis for Nike. A man in a black shirt appears distressed, visually representing the initial shock, while the narrator explains that "Phil thought it must be a joke or some strange mistake." This is followed by a man in a suit in a dark room, suggesting a tense corporate environment. The scene shifts to a bustling office with employees packing boxes, indicating layoffs or restructuring, while a man in a light blue shirt walks through the space, reinforcing the sense of corporate upheaval, as the narrator somberly states that owing "$25 million dollars to the government, they'd be out of business... everything they'd worked for." A woman is shown on a phone call by a window overlooking a cityscape, with the text 'THE BILL WAS COMPLETELY REAL' appearing, directly echoing the narrator's confirmation that "it soon became clear that the bill was completely real," emphasizing the legitimacy of the financial threat. The camera then tilts up a towering skyscraper with the word 'SINISTER' in purple, visually aligning with the narrator's revelation that "the actual reason behind it was something surprisingly sinister," heightening the ominous tone. In a dark boardroom, silhouetted figures discuss strategy, with the Keds and Converse logos appearing, as the narrator explains that "Nike's American competitors, Keds, Converse, and a few small factories, were getting scared of Nike's popularity and growth," revealing the competitive origins of the crisis. This is followed by a man in a suit observing a computer screen displaying 'THE JAPAN THREAT,' suggesting a competitive business landscape, as the narrator clarifies that competitors "saw Nike as a threat that could potentially shut them out of the market." The scene transitions to a man in a suit in a glass-walled office, with a red 'STOP' sign overlay, symbolizing a halt to operations, as the narrator reveals that competitors "sent representatives to Washington and lobbied the customs office to effectively put a stop to Nike's growth by enforcing something called American Selling Price." Text overlays then explain the 'ASP' rule—an archaic pre-Great Depression regulation—detailing that importers of similar shoes must pay a 20% duty based on a U.S. competitor’s price, with the narrator providing the full context that "ASP was an archaic rule from before the Great Depression... anyone importing a similar shoe from abroad must pay an import duty of 20% of the competitor's selling price." The video concludes with a shot of a government building labeled 'NATIONALISM' and a man in a suit inspecting a vintage car, tying the financial and regulatory conflict to broader themes of American industry and protectionism, as the narrator explains that "the whole idea behind this law was to promote nationalism and essentially prevent companies from import..." The entire segment is narrated by a single male voice, maintaining a serious, informative, and slightly ominous tone.

segments.integrated_caption: The scene opens in a dimly lit boardroom where men in white shirts and ties, representing major footwear brands like Keds and Converse, are engaged in a serious meeting, as a male narrator, accompanied by a light, investigative synth background music, explains how Nike's competitors lobbied the federal government to enforce an old rule about manufacturing products in America. The visual transitions to an aerial view of a bustling shipping port filled with colorful cargo containers, serving as a backdrop for animated graphics. A purple silhouette of the USA and Japan appear, followed by a purple Nike sneaker and green dollar bills, symbolizing international trade and financial stakes. Text overlays announce 'AMERICAN ALTERNATIVE' and 'IMPORT FEE,' with a ship icon moving between the two countries, culminating in a bold '$6 ON EVERY PAIR THEY IMPORTED' message, as the narrator details how Nike's manufacturing in Japan led competitors to create expensive alternatives and how Phil would face an additional $6 fee per imported pair. The narrative shifts to a close-up of a man’s face, overlaid with a timeline of shoe imports from 1971 to 1979, marked by red and purple shoe icons, visualizing the history of import fees, as the narrator explains Nike's imported products were hit with high fees due to the 'American selling price rule.' A large check for $25,000,000 from Deutsche Bank New York is shown, which the narrator reveals was a backdated import duty bill. The scene then shows a man in a suit with a determined expression, followed by a man in a light blue shirt with a loosened tie, looking distressed, over whom the bold yellow text 'FIGHT' appears, emphasizing confrontation and determination, as the background music grows more dramatic and the narrator explains Phil knew Nike couldn't pay the bill and had to 'fight,' hiring a top lawyer to fight the customs office and competitors like Converse and Keds for three brutal years. The scene cuts to a dimly lit courtroom with a man in a suit standing among others, directly aligning with the narrator's description of Phil fighting in court. Finally, a woman in a white and black athletic outfit stands confidently on a soccer field, followed by a wide shot of the field with a ball being kicked, symbolizing athletic competition and resilience, potentially part of a TV commercial Nike produced to tell their story.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with a dynamic, layered montage blending scenes of a man in a black Nike outfit crouching on a track, overlaid with a crowd, as a male narrator explains the company's efforts to get customers "fired up and on their side," which "worked." The scene transitions to a modern office setting where two men in business casual attire engage in conversation, then to a corporate boardroom where a man in a suit sits at a glass table, surrounded by colleagues, as large yellow text overlays announce '$9,000,000'. The narrator details how Nike's competitors and government accomplices realized Nike would "fight this with everything they had," leading to a settlement of $9 million instead of $25 million, which Phil still thought "was outrageous." As the text '$369,709,846' overlays the boardroom, the narrator highlights that despite the legal battle, Nike had "grown tremendously," with sales reaching "a whopping $440 million," allowing them to settle and put the issue behind them. The narrative then moves to a courtroom where a man in a suit gestures passionately, a hand writes on a whiteboard with red marker, and a man in a white shirt holds a 'DUNKIN' DONUTS' cup, looking contemplative. A man in a suit observes a cityscape through a window, with text declaring 'IT WAS TIME FOR SOME VERY GOOD NEWS', as the narrator concludes that it "had been another close scare, but the company had now made it through stronger than ever," and "it was time for some very good news." After a brief silence and a dramatic sound effect, a black screen transitions to a title card reading 'CHAPTER 8: GOING PUBLIC' over sketches of sneakers. This is followed by a cityscape with '1980' in bold purple text, where a man with white hair, in a suit, states, "WE EFFECTIVELY KILLED CASH FLOW PROBLEMS." The narrator confirms that "In 1980, Nike became a public company, which effectively killed its cash flow problems completely, and allowed them to ramp up their growth even more." The segment concludes with a man in a suit, identified as Phil, with arms crossed against a wall featuring a large Nike swoosh, as text reveals 'PHIL ALONE OWNED A 46% SHARE OF THE COMPANY'. The narrator emphasizes, "And since Phil alone owned a 46% share of the company, he became a very wealthy man." The lighting shifts from warm office tones to cooler, more dramatic courtroom and cityscape hues, reflecting the evolving narrative from internal strategy to public triumph.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with a close-up of a man in a plaid shirt and glasses in a serious conversation, as a male narrator, accompanied by an uplifting instrumental track, recounts how the parents of one of Nike's early employees invested their life savings. This is visually represented by a man in a beige shirt and glasses holding a small box in a brightly lit convenience store, with the on-screen text 'MY LIFE SAVINGS.' The narrator explains this wasn't for profit but 'to try and keep the company alive,' aligning with a dark, purple-tinted scene featuring the corresponding text. A man in a beige shirt and tie confidently presents in a corporate boardroom with a factory view, symbolizing this struggle for survival. The scene shifts to a White House press briefing on September 15, 2008, setting a backdrop of national economic crisis, before cutting to a dimly lit office where a group of people focus on a computer screen. The narrator reveals the dramatic turnaround: 'their initial $8,000 investment was now worth $1.6 million,' a moment of success echoed by a woman in a polka-dot blouse and glasses smiling at a laptop. As the narrator states Nike 'became bigger and bigger,' a bustling office and a formal press conference in Korea with a woman speaking at a podium illustrate the company's growth and international expansion into China. A dramatic glowing, purple-outlined silhouette of a man in a Nike shirt appears, symbolizing transformation. A man in a suit holding a cigar looks out at a cityscape, with the text 'WE COULD NOW OFFER YOU MORE THAN JUST SHOES,' as the narrator explains Nike branched out into selling clothes and could offer more than just footwear. The segment highlights Nike's strategy of celebrity endorsements, showing a split-screen of LeBron James and Tiger Woods in Nike apparel, with the text 'IN THE VERY EARLY STAGES OF THEIR CAREER,' as the narrator details their deals with these 'rookie athletes.' The video concludes with a young man in a white 'BULLS' jersey celebrating on a basketball court amidst cheering fans, symbolizing the massive boost Nike received from these stars.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with a dramatic close-up of Michael Jordan in a Bulls jersey, holding a basketball, set against a backdrop of his name repeated in bold gold letters, as a male narrator explains that Nike's most lucrative endorsement was with Michael Jordan. The scene transitions to a high-energy basketball game, where Jordan, now in a black Bulls #23 jersey, leaps for a shot surrounded by opponents, as the narrator details how Nike spotted his potential early and signed him for a sponsorship deal. A stylized shot of a Nike Air Jordan 1 sneaker engulfed in flames against a city skyline follows, symbolizing the brand’s explosive rise, while the narrator explains that as Jordan rose to stardom, his Air Jordans shoe line made hundreds of millions of dollars. The narrative then shifts to a purple-tinted map of the United States, with the Nike swoosh and Converse logo positioned on opposite sides, hinting at a competitive rivalry, as the narrator states that by 1986, Nike had overtaken Converse to become the biggest sports brand in America. A man in a suit appears in a boardroom, delivering a serious statement, with the text 'NIKE IS SIMPLY AN INCREDIBLE BUSINESS SUCCESS STORY' overlaid, reinforcing the brand’s triumph, as the narrator suggests viewers might perceive Nike as an incredible business success story. The tone darkens as a mountain emerges from water, with the text 'BUT HERE'S THE THING' and 'DARK SECRET' appearing, leading to a critical shift, as the narrator ominously reveals that behind all that success was a very dark secret. This leads to a montage of children in a dimly lit factory setting with the words 'CHILD SLAVERY' and 'CHILD LABOUR' in bold, exposing the ethical underbelly of the brand, as the narrator explicitly states the secret involved "violent intimidation, slavery, harassment, and child labor," and was "about to be exposed." The segment concludes with a 'QUICK BREAK' title card, as the narrator announces it's time for a quick break and asks for a favor. This is followed by a YouTube interface for 'MagnatesMedia,' urging viewers to subscribe and enable notifications, with a hand clicking the bell icon, as the narrator requests viewers to "turn on the notification bell for MagnatesMedia" if they are enjoying the video. A silhouette of a man raising his arms in triumph appears, with the text 'TURN ON NOTIFICATIONS' overlaid, as the narrator explains he posts mini-movies about business, money, and marketing, and encourages viewers to turn on notifications to ensure they see new content, promising to "love you forever." The final frame introduces 'CHAPTER 9: THE DARK TRUTH' with sketches of sneakers, signaling a deeper, more critical exploration of the brand’s history, accompanied by a distinct click sound.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with a dramatic sound effect as a title card appears, reading 'CHAPTER 9: THE DARK TRUTH' in bold black text on a white background, flanked by purple sneaker sketches, setting a serious and investigative tone. A male narrator begins, stating that in 1991, American labor activist Jeffrey Ballinger published a report that nearly destroyed Nike's reputation. The scene transitions to a dimly lit shoe factory where workers are engaged in production, with one man in a tan jacket holding a shoe, illustrating the manufacturing focus. A man in a suit with a name tag 'BALLINGER' and a 'Warren' pin smiles against a backdrop of golden stars, representing the corporate figure Ballinger, who claimed Nike secretly used child labor and exploited people in underdeveloped countries. The narrative shifts to a sepia-toned historical scene of a man in a suit speaking into a microphone, followed by a group of uniformed individuals standing in formation in a dusty courtyard, with purple overlays of country names—Uganda, Bangladesh, Cambodia—as the narrator details how workers in these countries were forced to live in slums near open sewers, sharing toilets and bathwater with over 100 different families. The visuals become increasingly grim, showing a dark, dilapidated interior with yellow toilet and shower icons, then a family icon with '100+' below, emphasizing the overcrowding and lack of sanitation. A distressed woman in a military-style cap appears, with red-circled icons of a water tap and toilet, reinforcing that workers were denied access to drinking water or toilets during their work hours. The scene cuts to a dark silhouette, then to a city skyline with '$1.25' in yellow, highlighting the meager sum of $1.25 these workers were paid. A glitchy, purple-tinted graphic displays '2001', 'CAMBODIA', and '7 DAYS A WEEK, 16 HOURS A DAY', as the narrator reveals that in 2001, a BBC documentary uncovered child labor and poor working conditions in a Cambodian factory used by Nike, focusing on six girls who worked 7 days a week, 16 hours a day. The segment ends with a red-tinted shot of a news anchor on a TV screen, with 'CNBC EXCLUSIVE' and 'MARIA BARTIROMO' visible, suggesting media exposure of these issues and the backlash they caused.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with a chaotic, red-tinted protest scene, as a narrator describes "students and activists around the world participating in protests against Nike," with calls for boycotts and universities cutting ties. Visually, a man in a dark suit, identified by the narrator as Phil Knight, walks through an ornate gateway, symbolizing his entry into a corporate environment. The narrator explains that "sales dropped, and Nike stock fell by 15%," coinciding with a computer screen showing a stock chart's sharp decline marked '1,000,000'. A vintage television then displays a news anchor with a 'FORCED LABOUR' graphic and the Nike logo, while the narrator details how the company was "portrayed in the media as a company willing to exploit workers and deprive them of their basic wages needed to sustain themselves," echoing the on-screen text. Phil Knight is seen shaking hands with a crowd, then reflecting by a window, and finally, in a dimly lit room, making a commitment, "I would personally ensure conditions at Nike factories around the world improved," a promise also shown as a text overlay. The scene transitions to a world map with magnifying glasses over Asia and Africa, indicating global investigation into labor practices. A man in a red shirt and hat is seen smoking in a jungle setting, possibly a field investigator. A man in glasses speaks seriously, with text stating 'THEY ARE TRYING TO MINIMISE THESE PROBLEMS', as the narrator mentions "human rights activists have acknowledged their efforts to minimize these problems." The segment concludes with a document featuring foreign text and a factory icon displaying '50%' and 'MAKE LESS THAN MINIMUM WAGE', as the narrator refers to a "2005 report by the company" finding workers in "up to half of its" factories.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with bold purple text, 'MAKE LESS THAN MINIMUM WAGE' and 'DENIED USE OF BATHROOMS AND DRINKING WATER,' as a narrator states that factories were "forced to work 60-hour weeks, made less than minimum wage, or were denied the use of bathrooms and drinking water." A man in a dark suit is then seen reading documents in an office, suggesting an investigation. A close-up of a computer screen reveals a news article from the Human Rights Resource Centre about 'Human rights violations off the rack,' naming Nike, Patagonia, and C&A, and mentioning forced labor in China’s Xinjiang region, which the narrator elaborates on, explaining that a criminal complaint was filed against Nike and other brands for benefiting from forced labor. The Man in Suit engages in a serious conversation, gesturing with a pen, while another man in a blue shirt walks past a glass door labeled 'WASHOE MINING CORPORATION.' The tension escalates with bold yellow text 'THAT IS NOT AN EXCUSE!' appearing on screen, as the narrator emphatically states, "And whilst that's not an excuse!" The scene shifts to a distressed Man in Suit in a dimly lit, gritty environment, followed by a chaotic outdoor scene of workers in a jungle-like mining or factory camp with makeshift structures. A close-up of a young man’s dirt-covered face conveys the harsh conditions. The narrator then broadens the scope, explaining that "countless multinational companies use overseas labor with very questionable conditions." The video cuts to a YouTube interface displaying various investigative videos, including 'The Dark Secrets Elizabeth Holmes Was Hiding,' as the narrator reflects that looking into "almost any business deep enough, you're going to find a darker side." The final frames show two men, one with binoculars and another in a cowboy hat, observing from a bridge, followed by a purple-tinted Nike logo with the text 'WHAT'S PARTICULARLY INTERESTING ABOUT NIKE,' which the narrator echoes, asking "what's particularly interesting about Nike." The segment concludes with a man in a blue shirt standing over a massive pile of cash in a warehouse, symbolizing the financial stakes of these labor issues, all underscored by a tense, investigative background music.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with a dark, ominous shot of a man in a dark suit standing over a massive pile of cash in a warehouse, as a narrator concludes a thought about turning "controversy into profits" over suspenseful, deep background music. The scene transitions to a title card, 'CHAPTER 10: TURNING CONTROVERSY INTO MILLIONS,' overlaid on a cityscape with sneaker sketches, as the music shifts to a more dramatic, building orchestral sound. The narrator then explains that "In 2018, Nike launched a new ad titled Dream Crazy with former NFL player Colin Kaepernick as the voice and face of its new Just Do It campaign." The video shows Kaepernick smiling, then in a close-up with the ad's tagline 'Believe in something. Even if it means sacrificing everything.' and the Nike slogan 'Just do it.' The narrator continues, detailing how "Kaepernick himself posted a black and white close-up photo of his face with the words, 'Believe in something, even if it means sacrificing everything.'" This statement, the narrator clarifies, "was in reference to a controversy that began back in 2016, when Kaepernick himself triggered a political scandal by kneeling during the US National Anthem to protest against racial injustice and police brutality in America." A news article titled 'Kneeling, Fiercely Debated in the N.F.L., Resonates in Protests' shows Kaepernick kneeling during a game, followed by a graphic of a raised fist with 'RACIAL INJUSTICE.' The narrator adds that "Many people, including then-President Donald Trump, claimed that Kaepernick was disrespecting the flag and the country," aligning with a Fox News tweet quoting 'You cannot disrespect our country.' The scene shifts to two men in suits walking and talking, with the bold word 'CONTROVERSIAL' appearing, as the narrator confirms, "So, Nike's partnership with Colin was controversial to say the least." A man in a plaid shirt and cap in a store is shown with 'STOCK PRICE IMMEDIATELY' overlaid, as the narrator reveals, "Their stock price immediately fell 2.5%." A tweet shows a Nike sneaker being burned with the text 'Since when did the American Flag and the National Anthem become offensive?', illustrating how "many people across the United States destroyed their Nike shoes and declared a boycott of the company." The video ends with a blurred image of a hand holding a burning sneaker, overlaid with 'BUT THEN SOMETHING INTERESTING HAPPENED,' and a young man in a suit walking through a crowded office, as the narrator states, "But then, something interesting happened. A lot of people who shared the same sentiment behind the theme of Nike's..." fading out, hinting at a shift in public perception or business outcome.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with a dynamic, fast-paced sequence of a young man in a light blue shirt and backpack running intensely through a crowded urban environment, set to a subtle, driving electronic beat. A close-up of a white Nike sneaker being laced up emphasizes the product, as a male narrator explains how many people started buying and wearing Nike products as a show of support. The scene shifts to a man in a black jacket with 'WORLD RECORD' before a large crowd in an industrial alleyway, while the narrator details how Nike gained more attention than ever through controversial marketing, breaking many records, achieving $6 billion in brand value, $163 million in media, and an additional 31% bump in sales. Bold, animated text overlays like 'AND BROKE MANY RECORDS' and '31% BUMP IN SALES' appear on dark, smoky backgrounds, followed by a golden Emmy statuette and 'EMMY AWARD SINCE 2002', as the narrator confirms Nike earned its first Emmy Award since 2002 for the most outstanding commercial of 2019. A Nike store exterior is shown, then a man in a suit walks past a 'NO SMOKING' sign, with text stating 'HAD LITTLE SIGNIFICANCE OR ANY MAJOR IMPACT ON ITS SALES', aligning with the narrator's point that boycott shouts had little impact on sales. The narrator then explains Nike's history of leaning into controversies for profit, as a city skyline with '1988' appears, followed by the Nike logo and 'JUST DO IT CAMPAIGN'. segments.integrated_caption: The segment begins with a split-screen featuring Colin Kaepernick and the Nike slogan 'Believe in something. Even if it means sacrificing everything.' and 'Just do it.' on the left, juxtaposed with a man looking down in a dimly lit room on the right, overlaid with yellow text: 'THEY WEIGHED UP THE COSTS AND BELIEVED THAT THE BENEFITS WERE WORTH IT.' A male voiceover immediately explains that "they weighed up the costs and believed that the benefits were worth it," attributing the company's success largely to ad campaigns. The visuals transition through a man in a suit at a desk, then a glitchy billboard in a Japanese city displaying Nike sneakers and Japanese text, as text overlays detail the 'JUST DO IT' campaign's launch from 1988 to 1990 and its impact on sales, which the voiceover confirms grew from $800 million to $9.2 billion by 1998. The narrative shifts to a man in a suit, seen first in an office, then looking tired on an airplane, as the voiceover reveals "Phil" stepped down as CEO after 40 years, describing "His story is pretty remarkable." A woman in a flat cap appears with the text 'HIS STORY IS PRETTY BIGGEST,' visually accompanying the voiceover's account of Phil's journey from a kid with no experience to building the world's biggest sports brand. Subsequent shots include a man in a white shirt flexing in a vintage car, the man in a suit at a diner, and another man in a dark suit in a dimly lit room, while the voiceover prompts viewers about their own "crazy idea" and the necessity of a "leap of faith." The video culminates with the iconic Nike logo and 'JUST DO IT.' on a dark background, fading to black, as the voiceover delivers the brand's famous tagline, "just do it," before an outro for further content. A consistent, slightly dramatic background music underscores the entire segment.
